content.problemStatement:
**This is the hard version of the problem STDIFEZ. The only difference between them is the constraint on NNN: here, NNN and the sum of NNN won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.**

Suppose you have an array AAA of length MMM. Every element of AAA is either 111 or 222.

Consider another array BBB of length MMM. Initially, Bi=0B_i = 0Bi​=0 for all iii.

You can perform the following operation:


Choose an index iii (1≤i≤M1 \leq i \leq M1≤i≤M) that was not previously chosen.
Then,

If Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1, add 111 to BiB_iBi​.
If Ai=2A_i = 2Ai​=2, add 111 to all of Bi−1,Bi,B_{i-1}, B_i,Bi−1​,Bi​, and Bi+1B_{i+1}Bi+1​.

The addition is done only to valid elements: particularly, if Bi−1B_{i-1}Bi−1​ and/or Bi+1B_{i+1}Bi+1​ don't exist due to their respective indices not falling in [1,M][1, M][1,M], the corresponding elements are ignored.


AAA is said to be stable if the array BBB can be made a palindrome after performing a **positive** number of the above operation.

You are given an array AAA of length NNN, each element of which is either 111 or 222.

Count the number of non-empty contiguous subarrays of AAA that are stable.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the number of non-empty contiguous subarrays of AAA that are stable.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The subarrays [1][1][1] and [2][2][2] are stable, because choosing i=1i = 1i=1 gives B=[1]B = [1]B=[1] for both of them, which is a palindrome.
The subarray [1,2][1, 2][1,2] is also stable, since choosing i=2i = 2i=2 gives B=[1,1]B = [1, 1]B=[1,1] which is a palindrome.
Test case 222: The stable subarrays are:

[1][1][1] and [2][2][2], as seen in the previous sample. There are two occurrences of the former and three of the latter.
[1,2][1, 2][1,2] stable, with two occurrences.
Similarly, [2,1][2, 1][2,1] is stable, with two occurrences.
[1,2,1][1, 2, 1][1,2,1] is stable, since choosing i=2i = 2i=2 results in B=[1,1,1]B = [1, 1, 1]B=[1,1,1], which is a palindrome.
Similarly, [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2] is also stable, since choosing i=1,2,3i = 1, 2, 3i=1,2,3 results in B=[1,3,1]B = [1, 3, 1]B=[1,3,1] which is a palindrome. It has two occurrences.
[2,1,2,1,2][2, 1, 2, 1, 2][2,1,2,1,2] also stable.

The total number of stable subarrays is thus 2+3+2+2+1+2+1=132+3+2+2+1+2+1 = 132+3+2+2+1+2+1=13.
